retrieved_docs.title: Glass Ionomer Cement -- Issues of Concern -- Properties of Metal Modified GIC
retrieved_docs.content: Chemical bonding to the tooth structure is slightly lessened due to the silver present in the composition. Both silver alloys admixed GIC and cermet release fluoride in significant quantities. However, the fluoride release is comparatively less in cermet than in silver alloy admixed GIC. Metal-modified GICs have improved handling properties and exhibit fewer porosities in set cement.

retrieved_docs.title: Heavy Metals -- Etiology and Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Mercury (Hg): The most common source of mercury is methylmercury, found in fish secondary to pollution. [11] In adults, cases of methylmercury poisoning are characterized by the focal degeneration of neurons in brain regions such as the cerebral cortex and the cerebellum. Depending on the degree of in-utero exposure, methylmercury may result in effects ranging from fetal death to subtle neurodevelopmental delays. Consequently, because pregnant women, women of childbearing age, and young children are particularly at risk, the FDA recommends that they avoid eating shark, swordfish, mackerel, and tilefish. [12] Mercury is also an occupational hazard for dentists in countries where amalgam production (filling material for cavities) is still allowed. [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Glass Ionomer Cement -- Issues of Concern -- Metal Modified GIC
retrieved_docs.content: Metal particles in the silver alloy are added to the conventional GIC powder to improve the mechanical properties of GIC and permeate its use in Class I cavities in molar teeth. The GIC can be reinforced with metal in a silver admixture or cermet mixture.

Preferably, samples should be kept refrigerated. [24]

retrieved_docs.title: Glass Ionomer Cement -- Issues of Concern -- Cermet Mixture
retrieved_docs.content: McLean and Gasser introduced cermet in 1985. Cermet is obtained by sintering the microfine silver alloy with glass particles at high temperatures and pressure. The pellet thus formed is ground to form finer powder particles. [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Glass Ionomer Cement -- Issues of Concern -- Reinforced Cement
retrieved_docs.content: Highly viscous conventional glass ionomer cement: mainly useful for ART. The highly viscous GIC can be inserted in the cavity, similar to dental amalgam. [21]

retrieved_docs.title: Acrodynia -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Acrodynia is related more often to the elemental form of mercury (quicksilver) inorganic salts than the organic forms. [3] The elemental form is either liquid or vapor and is widely used in thermometers, blood cuffs, batteries, dental amalgams, fluorescent light bulbs, etc. The inhalational toxicity of quicksilver is more severe than that of oral toxicity. The organic forms of mercury can exist as short-chain, alkyl-mercury compounds (methylmercury and ethylmercury) or long-chain, aryl-mercury compounds (methoxyethyl mercury and phenylmercury). In particular, the toxicity of organic forms is related to consuming contaminated seafood.

retrieved_docs.title: Heavy Metals -- Etiology and Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Medications and supplements can also be of concern. While supplements of essential metals can be necessary for patients with deficiencies, inappropriate use could lead to clinical manifestations. Other supplements may have unknown amounts of metals, such as the case with Ayurvedic medicines. One study found that 65% of Ayurvedic medicines contained lead, while one-third contained arsenic and mercury. [6] Other specific sources of common heavy metals are listed below:

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Caries Classification Systems -- Issues of Concern -- G.V. Black Classification of Dental Caries
retrieved_docs.content: The G.V. Black classification of dental caries lesions first introduced in 1896 and now outdated, is the most influential dental caries classification system. [2] This system is based on the anatomical site of the lesion and initially divided carious lesions into 5 classes; a sixth class was later added. (See Table 1. G.V. Black Classification of Dental Caries.) This classification system also suggested a cavity design for each class utilizing materials available at the time of publication, such as silicate cement, amalgam, and gold. These recommendations resulted in cavities that would be unnecessarily large by modern standards. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Impression Materials -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: 3. Setting reaction: The base paste containing hydrosilane-terminated molecules reacts with an accelerator paste containing siloxane oligomers with vinyl end groups and a platinum catalyst. Although no by-product is formed, a secondary reaction leads to hydrogen gas production. That is why it is recommended to wait at least 30 minutes rather than have a pitted cast. [19] 4. Advantages: Excellent dimensional stability - impressions can be stored or posted before casting. Elastic recovery Great accuracy Short setting time Good tear resistance Automix available Hydrophilized addition silicone has good compatibility with gypsum The impression can be cast multiple times without jeopardizing the details [20] 5. Disadvantages: Hydrophobic - the impression area must be dry to prevent inaccuracies in the impression [21] The sulfur present in the latex gloves and rubber dam may interfere with the polymerization of the base and catalyst. Hydrogen gas may lead to pitting in the cast.

retrieved_docs.title: Acrodynia -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: The diagnosis and management of acrodynia involve a high suspicion based on exposure and a multidisciplinary approach. Although various jurisdictions have imposed policies to curb mercury exposure, practitioners can encounter cases of acrodynia. There is a growing trend of intake of herbal and ayurvedic medicines, which may contain an abnormally high amount of mercury and its variants. Diagnosing acrodynia can be challenging, given various conditions mimicking the clinical presentation. The health care professional should emphasize an environmental survey, regular follow-up, and proper choice of chelating agents for better treatment outputs.

retrieved_docs.title: Dimercaprol -- Indications -- Mercury
retrieved_docs.content: Acute or chronic elemental mercury exposure with a whole blood mercury concentration or 24-hour urine concentration of ≥ 100 mcg/L Acute inorganic mercury poisoning when whole blood mercury concentration or 24-hour urine concentration is greater than or equal to 100 mcg/L

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2039
retrieved_docs.content: Iron in other combinations is classified as an iron antianemic preparation. Iron antianemic preparations are therapeutic agents used to replenish iron stores and treat iron‑deficiency anemia. Formulations described as “iron in other combinations” contain iron together with additional compounds or nutrients, enhancing absorption or providing complementary benefits while still serving the primary purpose of correcting iron deficiency. Recognizing these combination products as a subset of iron antianemic preparations helps clinicians select appropriate therapy for patients with anemia, especially when co‑existing deficiencies or specific tolerability issues are present.

retrieved_docs.content: Vitamin A is a central component of a wide variety of oral and topical formulations, most often appearing as an active moiety or active ingredient. Multivitamin products such as “Daily Multiple Vitamin w/ Iron Oral Tablet,” “MULTIVITAMINS/FLUORIDE 0.5 MG TAB, CHEWABLE,” “Strovite One Caplets,” “Centrum Specialist Prenatal Multivitamin,” “Amerix Multivitamin,” and “PNV‑Select” all list vitamin A (often as vitamin A acetate, retinol acetate, or beta‑carotene) among their active constituents, indicating its role in providing essential micronutrients. Specialized supplements—including “FolaGent DHA,” “FolaMed DHA,” “PreGen DHA,” and “[A11 ENVIRO‑PROTECT]” sublingual solution—also contain vitamin A alongside other vitamins (e.g., vitamin C, vitamin D, B‑complex vitamins) and nutrients such as fish oil, underscoring its inclusion in combined nutrient therapies.
